[Page 1]

Heterochromatin: Hiding from the remodeling machines Craig L. Peterson1,* 1Program in Molecular Medicine, University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Worcester, MA 01605, USA *Correspondence: craig.peterson@umassmed.edu https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2024.08.012

In this issue of Molecular Cell, Sahu et al.1 find that shielding heterochromatin from SWI/SNF chromatin remodelers is essential to maintain and epigenetically propagate pre-existing heterochromatin domains, whereas SWI/SNF action protects facultative heterochromatic regions from premature silencing. Within the interphase nucleus, chromosomes are organized into structurally distinct heterochromatic and euchromatic domains that are essential for the regulation of gene expression and the maintenance of genome integrity.2 Disruption of this domain organization can lead to developmental defects and diseases such as cancer. Heterochromatin is largely transcriptionally silenced and typically forms ‘‘condensed’’ foci localized to either the nuclear or nucleolar periphery. In contrast, euchromatin appears more ‘‘decondensed’’ and is transcriptionally active. A general feature of heterochromatin is that it contains both hypoacetylated histones as well as repressive histone marks, such as H3-K9me or H3K27me. These repressive marks provide binding sites for heterochromatin-specific effector proteins, such as homologs of Heterochromatin Protein 1 (HP1) that use a chromodomain to recognize H3K9me2/me3 at constitutive heterochromatin domains found at centromeres, telomeres, and genomic loci packed with repetitive sequences. Multivalent interactions among heterochromatin effector proteins are believed to drive a compact chromatin state that prevents accessibility by the transcription machinery. Furthermore, heterochromatin effectors, like HP1, can undergo liquid-liquid or liquid-solid phase separation (LLPS or LSPS) when bound to chromatin, and it has been hypothesized that these phase transitions are key for heterochromatin structure and function.3,4 But does heterochromatin repress transcription simply by forming condensed structures that are tucked away against the nuclear envelope? How do cells prevent the

spreading of heterochromatin into adjacent, euchromatic regions? Recent highresolution imaging studies indicate that some heterochromatin domains may actually be more dynamic than once believed, weakening simple condensation models.5 In this issue of Molecular Cell, Sahu and colleagues1 find that the hypoacetylated state of fission yeast heterochromatin shields these domains from ATP-dependent chromatin remodeling enzymes, which, if allowed access, promote nucleosome dynamics that destroy heterochromatin structure and function. Furthermore, chromatin remodelers protect euchromatic regions from being encompassed and silenced by heterochromatin (Figure 1). Thus, rather than simply blocking transcription factors, heterochromatin may have evolved to protect itself from remodeling machines that can rapidly convert these structures into a transcriptionally active state. In the fission yeast, S. pombe, constitutive heterochromatin is found at centromeres, telomeres, and the silent mating type locus.2 The nucleation of heterochromatin starts with the recruitment of an H3K9 methyltransferase, Clr4, which is a homolog of mammalian Suv39h. The Clr4 methyltransferase can both ‘‘write’’ the H3-K9me mark and use its associated chromodomain to ‘‘read’’ H3-K9me, and a ‘‘read-write’’ cycle has been found to be key for both spreading and epigenetic inheritance of heterochromatin domains.2 The methylation of H3-K9 also provides binding sites for HP1 homologs, such as Swi6, creating heterochromatic chromatin fibers. The high concentration of Swi6 provides a platform for assembly of other effector proteins, including the

SHREC complex, which is related to the mammalian NuRD complex.6 SHREC, like NuRD, harbors both an ATPase subunit that organizes nucleosomes and also a histone deacetylase (HDAC), Clr3. Notably, in the absence of the Clr3 HDAC, heterochromatin domains gain H3-K14ac, show enhanced nucleosome turnover, and lose H3-K9me and HP1, demonstrating that maintaining the hypoacetylated state of heterochromatin is essential for its structure and function.2 How does histone acetylation dissolve heterochromatin? Sahu and colleagues1 hypothesized that the aberrant H3-K14ac mark might promote recruitment of an SWI/SNF family chromatin remodeler (often called BAF complexes in mammals), as these complexes contain subunits with bromodomains that recognize histone acetyl marks. Consistent with this idea, inactivation of the Snf22 and Rsc1 subunits of the S. pombe SWI/SNF and RSC complexes partially restored H3-K9me and transcriptional silencing at the silent mating type locus in the clr3 mutant.1 These results are consistent with an earlier study that found that Clr3 HDAC activity prevented the RSC remodeler from establishing nucleosome-free regions within heterochromatin domains.7 Histone acetylation likely remains high at heterochromatin in the clr3 snf22 rsc1 triple mutant, illustrating that this active mark has little impact on heterochromatin function in the absence of remodelers. Furthermore, ChIP studies demonstrated that SWI/SNF remodelers are normally excluded from heterochromatin domains, but loss of the Clr3 HDAC leads to their illicit recruitment where they promote extensive nucleosome turnover, H3-K9me loss, and

[Page 2]

Figure 1. Schematic depicting that heterochromatin domains prevent access by SWI/SNF family chromatin remodelers, whereby SWI/SNF at actively transcribed loci prevents heterochromatin assembly

increased transcription. Remarkably, simply targeting SWI/SNF to heterochromatin domains by fusing an SWI/SNF subunit to the chromodomain of an HP1 homolog nearly destroys heterochromatin at the silent mating type locus, telomeres, and even a centromere. If shielding heterochromatin from remodelers stabilizes the silenced state, is the presence of a remodeler at a euchromatic locus sufficient to ward off heterochromatin assembly? The answer is a resounding yes! At telomeres, heterochromatin was found to spread far downstream into euchromatic regions in the absence of SWI/SNF remodelers, indicating that these enzymes are key for euchromatin-heterochromatin boundary activity. Strikingly, new domains of H3K9me3 also appeared in cells that lack SWI/SNF and RSC remodelers, and importantly, the increased levels of H3K9me3 led to Swi6 recruitment, which indicates formation of bona fide heterochromatin.1 Many of these newly formed heterochromatin domains correspond to loci that are known to transition to heterochromatin in response to developmental and environmental cues, indicating that SWI/SNF functions to shield these loci from their premature conversion. These

3168 Molecular Cell 84, September 5, 2024

data also suggest the intriguing possibility that developmental or environmental signals may act directly on SWI/SNF remodelers to switch these loci to the heterochromatic state. How do remodelers disrupt heterochromatin domains? When heterochromatin gains H3-K14ac and promotes SWI/SNF recruitment, nucleosomes show higher levels of turnover.1 In a simple model, SWI/SNF displaces H3-K9me nucleosomes, leading to replacement with unmodified histones. Extensive replacement would decrease the local concentration of interacting HP1 proteins, destabilizing the heterochromatic domain. However, it appears that this model may be too simplistic. In the clr3 rsc1 double mutant, histone turnover is reduced to what appears to be the background level associated with stable heterochromatin, but the silent mating type locus remains in a disrupted state, similar to that of the original clr3 single mutant. Thus, remodelers must have additional activities that lead to heterochromatin disruption. Previous work has shown that mammalian SWI/ SNF complexes can directly interact with HP1 proteins, so one possibility is that remodelers can directly displace these key heterochromatin effectors from nucleo-

somes.8 Alternatively, perhaps SWI/SNF displaces heterochromatin arrays from their nuclear periphery tethers, moving these loci to nuclear environments that have lower concentrations of silencing effectors, like the Clr3 HDAC. The action of remodelers may also be incompatible with the organization of heterochromatin arrays into structures that undergo LLPS or LSPS. In all likelihood, remodelers use either a combination or all of the above mechanisms to destabilize these silenced neighborhoods. Mechanisms that regulate the recruitment of remodelers to genomic loci seem essential for nearly every aspect of eukaryotic gene expression. In the case of gene activation, gene-specific transcriptional activators, including pioneer factors, can recruit remodelers that open chromatin for the transcription machinery.9 In some of these cases, linker histones seem to play the role of a ‘‘shielding’’ factor that prevents remodeler access prior to their recruitment.10 Previous work has shown that forced recruitment of mammalian SWI/SNF to polycombrepressed, facultative heterochromatin loci can lead to the rapid displacement of the PRC1 silencing effector and gene activation.11 Given that histone hypoacetylation is a general theme of heterochromatin, it seems likely that all flavors of heterochromatin may work very hard to shield themselves from the damaging impacts of remodeling machines. Proteasomes safeguard the plant stress granule homeostasis Qi Chen,1,2 Xiaoxin Chen,1,2 and Peiguo Yang1,2,* 1School of Life Sciences, Westlake University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China 2Westlake Laboratory of Life Sciences and Biomedicine, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China *Correspondence: yangpeiguo@westlake.edu.cn https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2024.08.011

In this issue of Molecular Cell, Xie et al.1 revealed that the proteasome is a constitutive component of plant stress granules (SGs), and that enhanced proteolytic activity is essential for efficient SG disassembly and plant survival during the stress response. Plants can respond to temperature change, osmotic stress, and other environmental stimuli. Stress granules (SGs) are reported to form in plants under these conditions.2 The roles of SG formation in plant survival and how SG dynamics are regulated in plant systems have yet to be explored. The assembly/disassembly dynamics of SGs differ depending on the duration and extent of the stress. SGs exhibit an initial pro-survival function upon acute stress by the sequestration of untranslated mRNA and translation factors into the SG, from which they could be released during recovery. However, chronic stress may lead to the persistence of SGs, leading to toxic effects on cells, coupled with a material state transition from liquid to solid. This could lead to the loss of crucial cellular factors due to the depletion of these factors in solid aggregates. Aberrant phase transitions of SGs have been implicated in various neurodegenerative diseases, including amyo-

trophic lateral sclerosis, Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, Huntington’s disease, etc.3 Hence, the fundamental characterization of SGs formed under multiple stress conditions is needed to further delineate the role of SGs, not simply regarding SG formation as ‘‘good’’ or ‘‘bad’’ for cell survival. Plants harness extraordinary power to inhibit protein aggregation. For example, the aggregation of polyglutamine-containing Huntingtin (HTT) protein is inhibited in plant systems, and Arabidopsis chloroplast stromal processing peptidase protein could inhibit disease-associated protein aggregation in human cells.4 Heightened proteolytic activity is related to longevity, and targeting protease-related pathways may show the potential for therapy.5 Thus, comparative studies from different model systems can yield novel insights into basic SG biology and potential therapies for human disease.

Due to the lack of established markers for SG in plants, Xie et al. first compared a list of SG proteins in Arabidopsis and found that RBP47, an ortholog of human TIA1, could reliably and robustly label SGs under heat stress. SGs formed in plant cells, similar to human SGs, are also dynamic and undergo disassembly upon stress withdrawal (Figure 1). Interestingly, the increase in temperature and duration of heat can prolong SG formation and lead to delayed clearance of SGs. Through time-course studies and immunofluorescence staining methods, the authors were able to establish the dynamics of SG formation in plant systems. Mammalian SGs contain hundreds of RNA binding proteins (RBPs) and thousands of mRNAs.6,7 There are over 4,000 RBPs in the human proteome,8 and only a fraction of them can be localized to SGs. Many RBPs localize to P-bodies and other condensates in cells. Thus,
